# Podcast interview transcript (simulated)

Host: So you often ask "compared to what?" — why?

Guest: Because binary questions hide the alternatives. "Is this a good idea?"
is usually unanswerable. "Is this a better idea than X, Y, or Z?" is tractable.
Every time I make a decision, I try to generate three alternatives and rank
them on the axis I care about.

Host: And when people say "this is obviously right?"

Guest: That is when I slow down. Obvious answers skip the falsification test.
Ask what would change your mind. If the answer is nothing, the belief is not
load-bearing on evidence.

Host: You also talk about exit paths.

Guest: Yeah. Before I commit to anything — a partnership, a feature, a bet —
I walk the exit. What does it look like to stop? If I cannot answer that, I
am not making a decision, I am closing a door.
